---
title: "A Practitioner's Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference"
subtitle: "Answer a handful of questions about your design and get an estimator recommendation with assumptions, caveats, an R package, and minimal code."
author:
  - name: Roberto Valli
    affiliation: European University Institute
date: today
---

## What this is

This site hosts an interactive **decision tree** that recommends a
causal-inference estimator for panel data. The tree branches on the
structure of the design: the number of ever-treated units, treatment
timing, whether the treatment is absorbing, whether it is binary or
continuous. Based on your inputs, you land on a leaf with the primary recommended method,
alternatives, identifying assumptions, caveats, the relevant R package,
and a minimal code snippet.

The app was built as a heuristic for students working on causal
inference with panel data, in response to the dizzying pace of the recent
literature on difference-in-differences-style estimators and their
extensions. **It is a work in progress** --- if you find a mistake, a
missing method, or have a suggestion, please
[open an issue](https://github.com/RobertoValli/practitioner_guide_did/issues).

## The decision tree

::: {.callout-tip}
## First load is slow
The tree runs entirely in your browser via WebAssembly. The first load
takes 10--20 seconds while R bootstraps; refreshing reuses the cache.
:::

```{=html}
<iframe src="tree/"
        style="width:100%; height:820px; border:1px solid #ccc; border-radius:4px;"
        loading="lazy"
        title="Panel-data decision tree (interactive)">
</iframe>
```

If the embedded version does not load, [open the app in a new tab](tree/).

## How to use it

1. **Answer one question at a time.** Each question narrows the menu of
   estimators based on the structure of your data. You do not need to
   know the methods ahead of time.
2. **Land on a leaf.** Each leaf returns the primary method, alternatives,
   identifying assumptions, caveats, an R package, and minimal example
   code.
3. **Use the breadcrumb at the top** to backtrack one step, or the
   *Start over* button to reset.
4. **Compare a few branches.** If your data structure is borderline (e.g.
   *almost* absorbing, *almost* common timing), walk both branches and
   compare the recommendations. Divergence is informative.

## What the tree branches on

| Question | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| **How many units are ever treated?** | One $\Rightarrow$ synthetic-control family. A few $\Rightarrow$ synthetic DiD. Ten or more $\Rightarrow$ standard DiD machinery. All units treated $\Rightarrow$ you need an extra source of variation (a baseline factor, an ineligible subgroup). |
| **Binary or continuous treatment?** | Continuous-dose designs require a different estimand (a dose-response curve, not a scalar) and different estimators. |
| **Absorbing or non-absorbing?** | Once-treated-always-treated unlocks Callaway--Sant'Anna, Sun--Abraham, Borusyak imputation, stacked DiD. Switchers / reversals require *fect* or DIDmultiplegt-style estimators. |
| **Staggered or common timing?** | Heterogeneity-robust estimators are required under staggered adoption; plain TWFE is unbiased only under common timing. |
| **Conditional or unconditional parallel trends?** | Doubly robust adjustments (Callaway--Sant'Anna with covariates, Sant'Anna--Zhao DR) when treated and control differ on observables that drive trends. |

When the design admits no DiD-style answer (no untreated contrast and no
extra leverage), the tree says so explicitly --- and points you toward
the relevant hybrid designs.

## Citing this tool

If you use the decision tree in teaching or applied work, please cite it as:

> Valli, R. (2026). *A Practitioner's Decision Tree for Panel-Data
> Causal Inference* \[online application\].
> <https://robertovalli.github.io/practitioner_guide_did/>

## Acknowledgements

The tree was developed alongside the EUI workshop *Advanced
Counterfactual Methods for Causal Inference with Panel Data* (Spring
2026). It draws on the recent econometrics literature, in particular
the synthesis by [Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski & Poe (2023)](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2023.03.008),
and on the implementation guidance shipped with the R packages it
recommends.

## License

The app and its content are released under the
[license in the repository](https://github.com/RobertoValli/practitioner_guide_did/blob/main/LICENSE).
